definition: A node that has received a message and will execute the compute() function in the current superstep. It becomes inactive after voting to halt and not receiving new messages.
text_evidence: Processing model is all active node will be executed whole processing completed when No more active node and No more in transit messages. The vertices become inactive after they vote to halt and there are no incoming messages to wake them up again.

definition: A user-defined mechanism to collect summary statistics or coordinate progress by aggregating values from all nodes (at partition and then global level) at the end of a superstep.
text_evidence: In addition, each node can also emit an "aggregate value" at the end of "compute ()". Worker will invoke a user-defined "aggregate ()" function that aggregate all node's aggregate value into a partition level aggregate value and all the way to the master. The final aggregated value will be made available to all nodes in the next superstep.

definition: A model of distributed computation where multiple processing units proceed in parallel in a sequence of supersteps. Within each superstep, units receive messages, manipulate local data, queue messages for other units, and synchronize at a barrier before the next superstep.
text_evidence: The execution model is based on BSP (Bulk Synchronous Processing) model. In this model, there are multiple processing units proceeding in parallel in a sequence of "supersteps". Within each "superstep", each processing units first receive all messages delivered to them from the preceding "superstep", and then manipulate their local data and may queue up the message that it intends to send to other processing units. ... When all the processing unit finishes the message delivery (hence the synchronization point), the next superstep can be started, and the cycle repeats until the termination condition has been reached.

definition: A user-defined associative and commutative function that combines multiple messages destined to the same node to reduce network bandwidth usage.
text_evidence: Messages destined to the same node can be combined using a user-defined "combine ()" function, which is required to be associative and commutative. This is similar to the same combine () method in Map/Reduce model.

definition: A graph parallel computing model designed to solve the problem of processing large graphs that exceed the processing ability of a single node.
text_evidence: With Hadoop and Spark, we can accomplish data parallel processing and improve the computing performance. How about big graph that beyond the single node processing ability. the graph parallel computing can solve this problem, represented by the Google Pregel, Apache Graph lab and Apache Giraph.

definition: A subset of the graph's nodes and edges, which is a unit of execution (typically with an associated thread) assigned to a worker machine.
text_evidence: The whole graph is broken down into multiple "partitions", each contains a large number of nodes. Partition is a unit of execution and typically has an execution thread associated with it. A "worker" machine can host multiple "partitions".

definition: A central coordinator that partitions the graph, assigns partitions to workers, signals the beginning of new supersteps, monitors worker status, and orchestrates fault recovery via checkpoints.
text_evidence: The "master" is playing a central role to coordinate the execute of supersteps in sequence. It signals the beginning of a new superstep to all workers after knowing all of them has completed the previous one. It also pings each worker to know their processing status and periodically issue "checkpoint" command...

definition: A system for large scale graph processing (think billions of nodes), and has served as inspiration for Apache Giraph and Apache Spark’s GraphX library. It can be thought as a generalized parallel graph transformation framework.
text_evidence: Pregel was first outlined in a paper published by Google in 2010. It is system for large scale graph processing (think billions of nodes), and has served as inspiration for Apache Giraph, which Facebook uses internally to analyze their social network, as well Apache Spark’s GraphX library, which provides an API for Pregel computations. Pregel can be thought as a generalized parallel graph transformation framework.

definition: A machine that hosts multiple graph partitions. It executes the compute() function on active vertices within its partitions, sends/receives messages, and reports status to the master.
text_evidence: A "worker" machine can host multiple "partitions". ... Each worker receive messages from previous "superstep" from its "inQ" and dispatch the message to the corresponding partition that the destination node is residing. After that, a user defined "compute()" function is invoked on each node of the partition.

definition: An alternative to message passing where sent messages are stored locally and only exchanged in bulk at the synchronization barrier between supersteps, rather than during computation.
text_evidence: An alternative to message passing is a state passing model in which the sent messages are stored locally and only exchanged at the end of a superstep. In other words, the model latches onto the synchronisation barrier between supersteps to exchange the state of message queues rather than during computation.

definition: A synchronized iteration in the BSP/Pregel model where all active vertices execute a compute() function, send messages, and then the system synchronizes before the next iteration.
text_evidence: The execution model is based on BSP (Bulk Synchronous Processing) model. In this model, there are multiple processing units proceeding in parallel in a sequence of "supersteps".

definition: In linear algebra, a factorization of a real or complex matrix that generalizes the eigen decomposition of a square normal matrix. Specifically, the singular value decomposition of an m*n complex matrix M is a factorization of the form U, where UΣV*, where U is an m*m complex unitary matrix, Σ is an m*n rectangular diagonal matrix with non-negative real numbers on the diagonal, and V is an n*n complex unitary matrix.

definition: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability: a set of properties that guarantee reliable processing of database transactions.
text_evidence: A database transaction has four ACID properties: A (Atomicity): Atomicity means that the transaction must be an atomic unit of work. For data modification, either all or none of them are performed. C (Consistency): Consistency means that when the transaction is completed, all data must be kept in a consistent state I (Isolation): Isolation means that changes made by concurrent firms must be isolated from changes made by any other concurrent firms D (Durability): Persistence means that after the transaction is completed, its impact on the system is permanent. This modification will be maintained even if a fatal system failure occurs

definition: The system ensures that clients can quickly read data and get a response within a reasonable time for every request, regardless of success or failure.
text_evidence: A stands for Availability, which means client can quickly read data, return operation results within a reasonable time, and ensure that every request has a response regardless of success or failure

definition: A NoSQL database transaction model characterized by Basically Available, Soft-state, and Eventual consistency, prioritizing availability over strong consistency.
text_evidence: The basic meaning of BASE is Basically Available, Soft-state, and Eventual consistency

definition: When part of a distributed system fails, the remaining parts can still be used normally, handling partition failures.

definition: A theorem stating that a distributed system cannot simultaneously meet all three requirements of Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance; at most, only two can be satisfied.
text_evidence: CAP Theorem means A distributed system cannot meet 3 requirements of Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance at the same time. At most, only two of them can be satisfied at the same time.

definition: In a distributed environment, all data across multiple storage nodes have the same data value at the same time, and all data backup updates are synchronized.

definition: A method that extracts all data in the entire source data storage every time, then converts it into a format recognized by the ETL tool and stores it in the target data storage.
text_evidence: Full extraction extracts all data in the entire source data storage every time, then Convert it into a format that can be recognized by the ETL tool and stored in the target data storage.

definition: A data incremental extraction method using an MD5 checksum: the ETL tool creates an MD5 temporary table for the source table, recording the primary key and an MD5 checksum calculated from all fields; during extraction, the source table's MD5 is compared to the temporary table's to identify changes.
text_evidence: The 4th Data incremental extraction methods is full table comparison. Full table comparison method Use MD5 check code: The ETL tool creates an MD5 temporary table with a similar structure for the table to be extracted in advance. The temporary table records the primary key of the source table and the MD5 check code calculated based on the data of all fields. Each time of extracting data, the source table and the MD5 temporary table are compared with the MD5 check code...

definition: A method that only extracts data newly added or modified in the source database since the last extraction.
text_evidence: incremental data extraction Extract new or modified data in the database since the last extraction, it normally would not have a big impact on the running business system.

definition: A data incremental extraction method that evaluates changed data through the database's own log; for example, Oracle CDC can identify changed data since the last extraction.
text_evidence: The first Data incremental extraction method- log comparison. This method Evaluate the changed data through the Database's own log. Oracle DBMS has a special component called changed data capture CDC, Oracle CDC can identify the changed data since last extraction;

definition: A data incremental extraction method where a timestamp field is added to the original table and updated each time the table data is modified; extraction is determined by comparing the last extraction system time with the timestamp field of each record.
text_evidence: The second Data incremental extraction methods is Timestamp. Add a timestamp field to the original table, and update the value of the timestamp field every time while modify the table data. When extracting data, determine which data to extract by comparing the value of the last extraction system time and the timestamp field of each record.

definition: A data incremental extraction method where triggers are created on the data table (e.g., for insert, modify, delete) so that when source table data changes, the changed data is written to a temporary table through the corresponding trigger; the extraction thread then extracts from the temporary table.
text_evidence: The 3rd Data incremental extraction method is using Triggers. Using this method, you can Create a trigger on the data table (for example, you can create three triggers for insert, modify and delete). Whenever the source table data changes, the changed data is written to the temporary table through the corresponding trigger. The extraction thread extracts data from the temporary table, Not the source table.

definition: Reports generated from analyzed data to provide insights, support decision-making, and monitor business performance, such as sales trends or operational efficiency.
text_evidence: Based on the data in the cluster, data scientist can generate the business intelligence report or other visualization graphs.

definition: The process of obscuring sensitive personal data (like names and IDs) within a dataset to protect individual privacy during analysis.
text_evidence: When data scientist analysis the data, they should mask the sensitive data like name , id etc. to protect the privacy.

definition: A storage solution designed to handle large volumes of big data by distributing it across multiple nodes or clusters.
text_evidence: due to the nature of big data, data volume is big, which may need distributed data storage solution.

definition: A centralized repository that stores curated, relevant data specifically for business intelligence and generating reports to support decision-making.
text_evidence: Sometimes, in order to do the effective analysis, data scientist would like to build an Enterprise Data Warehouse and move the curated data to the Enterprise Data warehouse, which just store the relevant data for the business intelligence. And on the basis of Enterprise Data Warehouse, analytics can generate the business intelligence report to support decision making.

definition: A process to extract data from different databases or data warehouses, transform it, and load it for further storage or processing.
text_evidence: Then collect data from the selected data sources, which could use the ETL(Extract Transform load) to extract data from different database or data warehouse or use data crawlers to crawl the data.

definition: The process of extracting or building representative key features from data to enable further modeling or algorithmic analysis.
text_evidence: After that, data scientist need to do the feature engineering, to extract or build the representative key features which can enable the further model or algorithm.

definition: A big data platform (cluster) used for storing and processing large volumes of data, often the destination for offloaded transaction and document data.
text_evidence: transaction data in database or data warehouse need to be offload & ETL processed to Hadoop, which is mostly the first step of journey.

definition: Data generated by Internet of Things devices, such as sensors and video cameras, used to monitor device status and environmental conditions.
text_evidence: With the help of the IoT device , we can collect IoT data from different kind of sensors, video cameras and so on , those data can help us learn the status of the devices and the its content.

definition: The period after 2010 where AI gives machines intelligence to analyze data autonomously, enabling wider and deeper analysis and more insights.
text_evidence: After 2010, the AI era has come and it has become the second sprout of the technological ecology after the Internet. AI give the machine some intelligence, which can analyze the data itself, which help people analyze the data and react correspondingly. This enable us analyze more widely and deeper consequently get more insight.

definition: A term describing large, hard‑to‑manage volumes of data – both structured and unstructured – that inundate businesses daily; it's not just about volume but also about being hard to manage by traditional means.
text_evidence: Big data is a term that describes large, hard‑to‑manage volumes of data – both structured and unstructured . Which is not only big volume, but also is hard to manage by the traditional way we used before.

definition: Data is compared to crude oil because original data cannot be used directly and must be processed before being applied, similar to how crude oil needs refining.
text_evidence: And we say Data is like Crude oil…., because normally, original data cannot be used directly, they must be processed before really being applied.

definition: The idea that decision‑making increasingly relies on data analysis; by connecting and processing diverse data sources, useful information can be extracted to support decisions.
text_evidence: New York Times 2012 special column: The era of big data is here, decision‑making relies more on data analysis. We have a lot of data from different aspects, connecting all this data and process it in the suitable way, we can extract useful information to support our decision making.

definition: Four key technologies that empower modern data‑driven capabilities: Big Data, Artificial Intelligence, Internet of Things, and 5G.
text_evidence: Four major technologies empower are Big Data, AI, IoT, 5G.

definition: A model where everyone can generate data (via blogs, social media, etc.) and also consume data.
text_evidence: In new model everyone could be the resource of data, all of us are generating data, and all of us are consuming data. we publish content in blog, twitter, Facebook, WeChat and YouTube, TikTok etc. and we also read the content there.

definition: A model where few companies (like newspapers, publishers, broadcast/TV stations) generate data and all others consume it.
text_evidence: in the old model Few companies are generating data, all others are consuming data. the few companies like newspaper agency, publishing house, the broadcast and TV station, which are the resource of data.

definition: Stage where sensors with processing capability (e.g., video cameras, wearable devices) automatically generate data 24/7, cited as the main reason for big data.
text_evidence: The 3rd stage‑Perception stage Sensor with processing capability, automatic; (7*24) generate data, like video camera, wearable device etc., which generate data all the time, no stop. ---This is the main reason for big data

definition: Data in table form with rows and columns, traditionally handled by conventional methods.
text_evidence: The traditional way we used to handle structured data, which is the data in table form with rows and columns.

definition: Early stage where data is generated by transaction systems as business activities occur (e.g., sales records in Walmart).
text_evidence: In early stage, the data generated by the transaction systems as the business activity happened. For example, when you buy something in Walmart, it would generate a sales record data in Walmart system.

definition: Stage where ordinary people can send messages and publish content in text, voice, and video via social media and mobile devices.
text_evidence: Then when the social media and mobile device developed, the ordinary people can send message and publish the content in text voice and video. So, it is the user‑generated content stage.

definition: Acronym for create, retrieve, update, and delete, representing the basic operations of a database that the data access layer supports.
text_evidence: Specifically, it provides support for the basic operations of CRUD (create retrieve update and delete) of the database.

definition: A software layer on top of the database that provides data exchange functions, enabling persistent storage and basic CRUD operations, transaction management, concurrent processing, and heterogeneous data conversion.
text_evidence: This introduces the data access layer. DAL is a layer of software that provides data exchange functions on top of the database. Its function is mainly to realize the persistent storage of application data, that is, write data into the database, and read data from the database and pass it to the application to realize data exchange.

definition: A common method for implementing the data access layer.
text_evidence: There are many ways to implement the data access layer, the common ones are data access object DAO (data access Object)

definition: The industry's earliest application programming interface to provide database access, using SQL as the database access language, and is essentially a set of database access APIs composed of function calls.
text_evidence: The industry's earliest application programming interface to provide database access is ODBC (open database connectivity), and the structured query language SQL is used as the database access language.

definition: Object-relation mapping, a method for implementing the data access layer.
text_evidence: implementation based on ORM (Object/relation Mapping)

concept_name: service data object (service data object) service middleware
original_name: service data object (service data object) service middleware
definition: A method for implementing the data access layer.
text_evidence: service data object (service data object) service middleware

definition: An interface based on a unified data model that supports access to cross-platform heterogeneous data in a distributed environment.
text_evidence: Unified data access interface is Based on a unified data interface to support Cross-platform heterogeneous data in a distributed environment

definition: The principle that data quality problems of different levels and scopes correspond to different data cleaning methods, and a clear data level range is the basis for finding a reasonable data cleaning method.
text_evidence: The data quality problems of different levels and scopes correspond to different data cleaning methods. A clear data level range is the basis for finding a reasonable data cleaning method

concept_name: multiple data sources - instance level problems
original_name: Multiple Data Sources - Instance Level Problems
definition: Data quality problems at the instance level for multiple data sources, including different representations of values and overlapping/duplicate records.
text_evidence: For the Multiple data resources data quality problems , in instance level it could be Different representations of values, Overlapping/duplicate records and so on.

concept_name: multiple data sources - model level problems
original_name: Multiple Data Sources - Model Level Problems
definition: Data quality problems at the model level for multiple data sources, such as naming conflict and structure conflict, which arise when integrating data from sources with different designs.
text_evidence: For the Multiple data resources data quality problems , in model level it could be Naming conflict, Structure conflict and so on.

definition: A model-level problem in multiple data sources where the same name is used for different data objects or different names are used for the same data object.
text_evidence: Naming conflict refers to using the same name for different data objects or using different names for the same data object.

concept_name: single data source - instance level problems
original_name: Single Data Source - Instance Level Problems
definition: Data quality problems at the instance level for a single data source, referring to errors and inconsistencies in the actual data content, such as spelling errors, redundant and repeated records, and attribute value conflict.
text_evidence: For the Single data resource data quality problems , in instance level it could be Spelling errors, Redundant and repeated records, Attribute value conflict and so on.

concept_name: single data source - model level problems
original_name: Single Data Source - Model Level Problems
definition: Data quality problems at the model level for a single data source, which depend on the integrity constraints of the design model on the data, such as uniqueness, integrity constraints, and attribute dependence.
text_evidence: For the Single data resource data quality problems , in model level it could be Uniqueness, Integrity constraints, Attribute dependence and so on.

definition: Four categories of errors for a single data resource: Attribute internal (single value), Inside the record (between attributes), Internal data source (between records), and Between data sources (inconsistency with other sources).
text_evidence:
Single Data resource data quality errors categories include
1) Attribute internal: limited to a single attribute value, such as age: 2000
2) Inside the record (between attributes): Different attribute values in the same record are inconsistent, for example, age and birthday cannot correspond
3 )Internal data source (between records): inconsistency between different records of the same data source, for example, the name of the same ID is inconsistent
4 )Between data sources: Some attributes in the data source are inconsistent with related values in other data sources, for example, the age corresponding to the same ID is inconsistent

definition: A model-level problem in multiple data sources referring to different ways to represent the same data object, such as different attribute granularity, composition structures, data types, or integrity constraints.
text_evidence: There are many different situations of structural conflicts, which usually refer to different ways to represent the same data object in different data sources. For example, the same object has different attribute granularity in different data sets, different composition structures, different data types, and different Integrity constraints, etc.

definition: Web content stored in search databases that responds only to direct queries, mostly structured data like academic, financial, legal, or governmental records.
text_evidence: Deep Web concept is created by Bright Planet in 2000, which is Used to express those websites whose information content is stored in the search database and only responds to direct queries; the content is mostly structured DB information. The information could be academic records, financial records, legal documents, government records and science reports.

definition: A method for deep web crawlers to associate HTML form fields with domains using heuristic rules, allowing input of domain-related parameters.
text_evidence: 4.1Based on domain knowledge: Use heuristic rules to associate the field of the form with the domain, thereby inputting parameters related to the domain concept.

definition: A method for deep web crawlers to iteratively obtain query keywords from sampled query results to maximize results with fewer queries, independent of domain knowledge.
text_evidence: 4.2 Domain-independent detection: Iteratively obtain query keywords from query results based on sampling, to obtain as many query results as possible with fewer queries

definition: Automated program designed to crawl deep web data from multiple sites, involving query interface recognition and form filling.
text_evidence: Construct a general deep web crawler in order to crawl the deep web data of many sites at once. 1）Query interface recognition: Use a variety of methods including visual layout to parse HTML forms or perform syntax analysis on HTML forms to automatically discover deep web data resources; 2) Add text similarity heuristic rules to associate HTML forms with specific fields to realize automatic filling of forms; 3 ) By constructing page classifiers and form classifiers to automatically find deep web databases related to tasks 4 ) Try to Automatically fill in the form.

definition: Volunteer computers on the Tor network that relay encrypted traffic to conceal users' locations.
text_evidence: Volunteers around the world provide about 5,000 computers as nodes on the transmission path to cover up new pages or chat requests. The Tor Project calls these points relay nodes.

definition: The acronym for 'the onion router'; a system enabling anonymous internet communication by routing traffic through multiple encrypted relay nodes.
text_evidence: Tor is the acronym for \"the onion router\", and users can communicate anonymously on the Internet through Tor. Tor sends chat messages, Google searches, purchase orders or emails through multiple computers in a circuitous manner, disguising the activities of Internet users like an onion wraps its core. Information transmission is encrypted at every step, and there is no way to know where the user is , Location and destination of information transfer.

definition: Algorithms that can be categorized into 4 groups: classification, clustering, correlation Analysis and anomaly detection.
text_evidence: Data mining algorithms can be categorized into 4 groups, classification, clustering, correlation Analysis and anomaly detection.

definition: A recent field that occupies the broader field of Machine Learning, most famous for its neural networks such as Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and Deep Belief Networks, which are modeled after the structure and functioning of the human brain's neurons.
text_evidence: Deep Learning is a recent field that occupies the much broader field of Machine Learning. Deep Learning is most famous for its neural networks such as Recurrent Neural Networks RNN, Convolutional Neural Networks CNN, and Deep Belief Networks. They are modeled after the structure and functioning of the human brain.

definition: The three types of layers that any deep neural network consists of: The Input Layer; The Hidden Layer and The Output Layer.
text_evidence: Any Deep neural network will consist of three types of layers: The Input Layer; The Hidden Layer and The Output Layer

definition: An algorithm used by computer systems to perform a task without explicit instructions, trained using data fed to the system to provide output, allowing computers to learn without an explicit need for programming.
text_evidence: A machine learning algorithm is able to do so with the help of data. Using the data fed to the system, a machine learning algorithm is trained to provide output to the users. Machine Learning is the scientific study of statistical models and algorithms that computer systems use to perform a task without explicit instructions. Basically, Machine Learning allows computers to learn without an explicit need for programming.

definition: A type of machine learning technique that enables an agent to learn in an interactive environment by trial and error using feedback from its own actions and experiences, using rewards and punishment as signals for positive and negative behavior.
text_evidence: Reinforcement Learning(RL) is a type of machine learning technique that enables an agent to learn in an interactive environment by trial and error using feedback from its own actions and experiences. Though both supervised and reinforcement learning use mapping between input and output, unlike supervised learning where feedback provided to the agent is correct set of actions for performing a task, reinforcement learning uses rewards and punishment as signals for positive and negative behavior.

definition: A machine learning method used to enable machines to classify objects, problems or situations based on related data fed into the machines, where the machines are repetitively fed data until they can perform accurate classifications.
text_evidence: Supervised learning is a method used to enable machines to classify objects, problems or situations based on related data fed into the machines. Machines are fed with data such as characteristics, patterns, dimensions, color and height of objects, people or situations repetitively until the machines can perform accurate classifications.

definition: A machine learning method used to enable machines to classify both tangible and intangible objects without providing any prior information about the objects, by exposing machines to large volumes of varied data and allowing it to learn and infer.
text_evidence: Unsupervised learning is a method used to enable machines to classify both tangible有形的and intangible objects without providing the machines any prior information about the objects. The main idea behind unsupervised learning is to expose the machines to large volumes of varied data and allow it to learn and infer from the data.

definition: An algorithm for frequent itemset mining and association rule learning over transactional databases.

definition: A deep learning architecture designed for processing structured grid data like images, using convolutional layers.

definition: Algorithms used to discover patterns and insights from large datasets.
